page: OVHcloud bm-m1 is a bare-metal server belonging to the Metal family. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs utilizing the x86_64 CPU architecture, running at a clock speed of 3.7 GHz. The system is provisioned with 64.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. Local storage is provided via a 960 GB NVMe SSD. The server operates on a KVM hypervisor and includes a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Additionally, the configuration includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU accelerators are present on this instance type.

The dedicated CPU allocation ensures consistent processing capabilities without resource contention. The high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 1:8 positions this server as a resource-dense option for memory-intensive workloads. However, the network bandwidth is limited to a baseline of 1 Gbps, which may act as a bottleneck for highly distributed systems or data-intensive networking tasks. The inclusion of a local NVMe SSD provides high-speed local storage, though capacity is fixed at 960 GB.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the bare-metal architecture eliminates virtualization overhead, providing direct hardware access and predictable performance per resource. This makes the server highly efficient for workloads that require dedicated, non-shared resources. The hardware configuration is optimized for database hosting, memory-intensive applications, localized virtualization environments, and general-purpose workloads requiring a balance of dedicated compute and high memory capacity.

Running on a KVM hypervisor, it delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps and includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. With dedicated CPU allocation and no GPU acceleration, this server offers consistent performance and high resource density. It is qualitatively cost-efficient for workloads requiring dedicated hardware without the overhead of shared virtualization. The hardware profile is well-suited for database hosting, memory-intensive applications, and localized virtualization tasks where predictable CPU and memory resources are required.
